speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Solomon Kupitz. And it-- today is April 25, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the couple was taken away. And the rest of them were taken away to the same grave where the grave was still open. And they were all shot to death. We went back to the houses, doing the same work, tailoring, shoemaking. I mentioned about a shoemaker-- there was a couple. The shoemaker's name was Velvel, a Russian Jew-- Ukrainian and his wife and two daughters. He was one of the best shoemakers. That's why the German Gestapo liked when he made the boots. And that's why they let him live. And so during March goes the work until June-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the beginning, actually, of June. Again, one night, my sister's still working over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: At headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: --at the headquarters. And one night, I was downstairs, like, in a cellar. And my sister says to my mother and my brother-in-law, my sisters, and my brothers, that to-- they told her that tomorrow they going to take all the children to be killed. And I am under the age of children, until 15 years of age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what can be done with me? So I don't exactly remember. My sister run out of the ghetto, wherever she went.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Ukrainian woman who-- her husband was in the Red Army and her son. She lived out of the town. And she spoke to her. And that Ukrainian woman came at 12 o'clock at night. She put her life also in danger. She could have been shot. And she took me to her house. As we came to her house-- as I came with her, they dressed me up not to be recognized. She put me-- like, there was a oven in the top between the oven where they bake, you know, I don't know, attic, you call it. And I was sitting there until 3 o'clock in the morning. She woke up. And she woke me up. And she said to me, we have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where do we go? We go 8 kilometers to Zhmerinka. Zhmerinka was a big ghetto of almost 4,000 or maybe 5,000 Jewish people. It was under the jurisdiction of the Romanian government.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because during the war, Hitler and Antonescu, which was the leader of Romania, gave up part of the Ukraine to the Romanians for recognition that they're fighting together. They're Axis. And this was a part of the Ukraine, which was Odessa up to Zhmerinka, other cities. The Romanians did not make big concentration camps. There were many Romanian Jews who were thrown out after the Germans and the Romanians took it over from the Russians-- part of Bukovina, Moldavia, and Bessarabia, not inside of Romania. But the Jews, which I mentioned-- the states were sending in to occupy Ukraine. And they call that Transnistria, which was Zhmerinka, only 8 kilometers away from Brailov. In Transnistria, in Zhmerinka, there was a ghetto. There was a hospital. There was a Judenrat. There was even a theater. There were Romanian Jews who were thrown out from Chernovitz and coming into that ghetto. Many died on the way coming over there. But everybody in that ghetto was working, working outside of the ghetto, helping the Romanians, doing the work, and also some occupation chairmans. There was no German SS or Gestapo authority over the ghetto, only Romanian authority. And this woman, Ukrainian, told me she's going to take me to Zhmerinka. We walked up from the house. She also told me-- because we had a river, small river, which was the Bug. They call it Bug. And there was a bridge. One side of the bridge was the Romanian side. And the other side was the Germans. And she knew exactly when the German guards are changing and the Romanians. She knew exactly. And we come very close to the bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am walking with her. I don't see nobody, only the whites, you know. I can mention also this, what I saw was a black cat crossing right us. And then I got scared. And I thought, who knows what's now going to happen? You thought that was bad luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad luck. But it was good look. And we crossed the bridge. And I had to go with her 8 kilometers. And she walked me up to the ghetto. There was wires-- electric wires. But she knew exactly where it was open. Because we couldn't come in through the main entrances because there were Jewish police watching the ghetto. So she knew where there was cut open under it, under the wires. And I got through. And she said goodbye to me. And I kissed her. And I came in the ghetto in the morning, early in the morning. It took me, I think, a nice couple hours. By 6 o'clock or 7 o'clock sometimes, I came in. I knew a name in the ghetto, a tailor. His name was Schmul Guz. I knocked on the door. He opened the door with his wife. They didn't have no children. And he let me in. As I came in, he told me to go to sleep. He saw how tired-- I told them everything. They did not know exactly what happened in Brailov. And I told them the whole story. They kept me. They gave me to eat. And I did not go out. Because he was afraid if I go out they're going to grab me and work, which they did not want this to happen to me. The Judenrat did not know that I came in. You had to report. And I did not want to report. I was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- at night, sometimes in the evening, I walked out with them. And I saw over there the head of the Judenrat. His name was Dr. Herschmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jew from Bukovina, Chernovitz. He was a lawyer before the war. He and his wife and other Romanian Jews, because they spoke Romanian, they knew their language. So they were the head-- they helped in that ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the middle of June, I knew I had to go out and get my family out from Brailov. In Brailov was left only maybe 40, not even that, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- I went to the head of the Judenrat. And I told them I have my family there. So he said, if you want your family, you'll have to have so much and so much in gold, gold money-- the Russian gold money, to give us. So I told him about my brother-in-law-- maybe he has. And if he'll come, he'll take care whatever he can. And he agreed with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my rest of the family, my mother-- I think-- I'm not sure if my sister first came. But my sister with her husband, my brother-in-law, then my mother, and my sister, Hannah and Bina, and Avraham, all came to Zhmerinka. Just one, Shaja, stayed over, the only one in Brailov. He didn't want to go. He worked-- they took him into the police station, the Ukrainian, because he was a very good tailor. And they wanted him to work there. And he did not come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the rest of my family came. And the only way that my brother-in-law got out and my sister was telling to the-- Graf, they told him that they go to Zhmerinka, but for a purpose to buy trimmings, all kind of trimmings-- cottons, needles, what they need for the tailoring. And they will come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he believed them. So when they came, they wouldn't go back. They knew what's going to happen to them. And my brother-in-law, matter of fact, opened a little also tailoring in Zhmerinka. And they start to work. And right away, he was a very cultured person and my sisters and my brother. And they became very friendly with the Romanian Jews. There were Russian Jews. There were all kind, but mostly were Romanians. And there was also a very famous theatrical group from Chernovitz. And a matter of fact, there was-- one of them was famous-- actresses, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], which was the most famous-- even up to today, I think one of them had grand-- grandchildren. Here in the United States was a famous actress in Hollywood, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then they had shows. And my brother-in-law was also, like, an actor. And they-- he liked them. And they became friends. Then, all of a sudden, which was in July-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not know, nobody knew that the Germans-- once in awhile, the Gestapo came to the-- close to the gates of the ghetto. We were living, actually, very close by the road of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw the SS men coming from Brailov and looking at the ghetto, just looking like the wolves. How can they kill them, too? But they couldn't because it was a Romanian authority. But they were looking for my brother-in-law and my sister. But mostly for my brother-in-law because he lied that he's going to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Couple weeks later comes a order from the Judenrat, Dr. Herschmann, that all the Jews-- that was the night before. He knew what's going to happen, but he did not tell nobody. But he told to some people that tomorrow morning, you're going to be waking up. You'll be coming in the-- in the center of the ghetto. And what's going to happen, he didn't say. He did not say nothing. We did not know anything, I mean, my family-- nothing. That night, my sister, Hannah, slept with her girlfriend in another building there. And I was with my mother and my other sister and my brothers. In the morning at 6 o'clock, in Jewish actually, it's-- was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], it's in July-- 18 days in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It was on a Friday in July. And it was-- a matter of fact, my mother didn't know. She was very, very religious. And she didn't eat that time because it was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That was before Tisha B'Av. You fast when there was destruction of the temple in Jerusalem. And she didn't eat that day until-- I remember it was a summer day. It was 10:00, after 11:00-- 11 o'clock because it's not dark enough. She didn't want to eat until so late. In the morning, when the Jewish police came knocking the door-- and they told us to go out. Everybody should be dressed in the best dressing what they have. We're going to be looked over as a medical mission came to check us out. That's all. And then we're going to go home. We went to the center of the ghetto. We sat down near a building-- used to be a factory. It was empty there. It was a red building. Across, there were all of us, 300 people, mostly young, young, young from, from children to the  oldest, maybe 40s-- 300 of us. The Romanian commander, with Dr. Herschmann from Judenrat, came with a big list and starts to read the names. And he saw-- here, my sister-- at that time, Hannah was sleeping at the house. So they told me to go, to wake her up, and tell her also to come out. And she came. And we were sitting on the ground. And everything was already written-- in the names. They told us to get up and go on the other wall, to stand up and stand on the other wall. As soon as we got up, the Romanian and German police gendarmerie circled us with machine guns from all around us, opened up the doors, pushed us in in that building. Then we knew this is going to be the end. They kept us inside until 2 o'clock. They brought in food with Dr. Herschmann and top nurses dressed in white. One or two girls, supposed to be my sister, to be changed her clothing another one dressed herself. And they got out with the food outside. And they survived. Dr. Herschmann told my brother-in-law, you try to be last one with your family. Maybe I can save you because you're a tailor. And my brother-in-law, at that time, was making a white suit for the commander, for the gendarmerie, the Romanian. So he told him that this is the tailor who's making your suit. And the shoemaker, Velvel, was making boots for this commander. As they start to come in and grab to 20-- a Romanian with the machine guns-- and grabbing to 20 people, they grabbed right away my older brother. And he is already out. I was little, small. So I pushed myself in the corner-- in a corner. I knew-- you know, in a-- in a moment like this, you know you're going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you try like a wolf to fight. And I was a fighter. And I stayed in the corner until they took out already most of the people, all of them, except my family and that shoemaker family, which was my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, when we were sitting on the floor inside, that shoemaker, Velvel, he knew that we have a father in America. And he grabbed my sister's long braids. She was blond. She didn't look even Jewish. And he kissed her and says, you'll never see your father again. And my mother was sitting right next to her. And then when they told us, the last one, to get into front. There were standing maybe 10 gendarmerie, the head of the commander, the Romanian, and Dr. Herschmann from Judenrat. And the Romanian commander said, who is that big family, on my family. So Herschmann said, this is Zynsztein, his wife, and his mother-in-law, and the children-- too big. He grabbed my sister right away with the braids, throw out my other sister. My brother, my mother were taken out. And as I was standing near my sister and near my brother-in-law-- and somehow I saw-- I don't know what happened in that moment-- but I think I grabbed the shoemaker because he had only two daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the commander said, who is this? So he hold me. And he says, this is my child. And I-- and then he went like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they walked out, the Romanian gendarmerie, all of them. And they left us in-- inside in the building, which was my sister, my brother-in-law, and I, the shoemaker, his wife, and the two daughters. And then they took them all to-- back to Brailov, to-- 20 people. Later on when I spoke-- the people of Zhmerinka in the ghetto, they saw everything. They did not touch them. They saw my whole family walking. They were crying, everybody. They saw my beautiful sister, Hannah. When they saw her walking-- she was a beauty, that the goyim used to-- they'll never forget how beautiful she was. And surrounded by police, gendarmerie, on the road in the middle of the way between Zhmerinka and Brailov, Gestapo already with the Ukrainian police came to approach them. And when they came-- also, somebody-- there was a man who had a girlfriend in-between the 300. He was a Zhmerinka man. And he was running after them in a side that they would not see him as far as to Brailov. He wanted to see what's going to happen to them. He was very brave. In the middle of the road, not far from Brailov, he saw a Romanian soldier taking my sister away. And he thought, maybe, he told her to get away and to run. But a half an hour later, he saw him taking her back, marching her back to the group. And he saw it. And then he told me this a couple days later. They were taken on the bridge, coming on that river, one side the Romanian, and the other side the Germans with the German Shepherds, with the dogs waiting already for their victims. Many of them start to scream on the bridge. They told them to get undressed on the bridge. There were one who used to be a furrier. He had a whole pack of gold. He throw it into the river. And the German Shepherd saw with the Ukrainian police. They ripped him apart. And the rest of the people are walking to the street, to their grave, naked, up to the grave. For them, they digged another grave already because over there was full. When they came to the grave and they came outskirts of Brailov, which was next to the big grave, they had-- yeah, my brother, who was still in Brailov was told by the Ukrainian police a night before that your family, they bring them all back. And this is because they run away. But you're going to survive. But we're going to kill them all tomorrow. But I'm going to try to give a easier way of dying-- your family. That was Baranchuk and Dubina telling my brother. When they were at the grave, they all kept, like, one bunch. They, they, they, they put themselves crying and screaming. They, they did not want to run into the grave. So they had to shot into the-- into the group. My sister tried to get out and to grab the gun from one of the SS men. And she was shot eight times. She was still alive. This is-- the Ukrainian people told me. They were not far away because they heard the screams. And the rest of my family, that Dubina, that savage, killed them. He sat on the side, so that he cannot see how they die. Then when they came to the police headquarters in the evening, he showed my brother his [NON-ENGLISH], his military coat full of blood. And he showed this to my brother, in prison there, in working down there. After that, when they were all killed, the night-- same day, I knew that, that there was shul in the ghetto. And I went to say Kaddish in the shul. And I said Kaddish a whole year. My brother, who was still in Brailov, the Ukrainian woman brought him over to Zhmerinka alive. He came. And I was in the ghetto doing hard work. And I stayed, in the beginning, actually, yeah-- I want to mention this. When I came out from the building with my sister and my brother-in-law, somehow, I didn't want to even go to their house. I had a-- some kind of a very bad feeling. I stayed away from everybody. I came back to the house where I was with my mother and the sister and my brothers. Empty, all alone-- I see their clothing. I see everything. And I was sleeping there. I didn't sleep. I was just looking at everything. Then after weeks went by, the-- Dr. Herschmann, the head of the Jewish Judenrat in Zhmerinka, mentioned to my brother-in-law he would have made a exchange. If he would have known that I will survive, he would exchange with my sister because she was so beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he would have her for himself because he was a cannibal. He had young woman. He had his wife, and he had others. That was a Jew. And that's what he mentioned. So that was already '42. And the Red Army already attacking Stalingrad. Germans came in, some Romanians. And we knew already news-- from Romania, came in news because they had contact from Romanian Jews came from Bucharest, whatever how they came.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were also ghettos around in that part of the Romanian Transnistria. [? Tolochin ?] was a very big concentration. Mogilev was Jewish. And the [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, the [NON-ENGLISH] Between Romania, Bessarabia, and the Ukraine, there were a big concentration of Jews in ghetto, mostly Romanian Jews. They were not killed. Many, many were killed in the beginning, but not regular liquidated in graves. But killed wherever they run, wherever they didn't work. There were Hungarian Jews coming in with certain Stars of David. There were doctors with the Hungarian Army. They were also savages. And somehow, they said they were going on the front line towards Russia. Meantime, Zhmerinka was a very big center of German concentration. Trains all over Russia because it was between-- going out from Russia towards Western Europe, Poland, and Romania, and Czechoslovakia. So I could see all the Germans running here and there. As soon as Stalingrad fell, the Germans start to run in a certain way back. That was already 1943. I was going to work-- slave workery-- working--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind. They took me-- actually, the Jewish police in the ghetto took me to work to load trains-- Romanian trains, German trains-- because I had mentioned-- was a big railroad center. So trains with the coal, trains with all kinds of things-- you had to put it in a wagon full of coal. And I was only with my brother and a couple boys. You had to do it so much and so fast. There were German soldiers shot also because Russian planes start to come in and bomb, tried to bomb one or two. The station and German soldiers fell dead. So SS, there was a lot SS. And they took me to, to put them into a grave. There were, maybe-- this was a good job, actually, for every German soldier to dig a second-- there was maybe 50 of them. As soon we finished to digging the graves, the Germans told us to get out. And they put them into the graves. They made speeches. So this was already action. Once the-- from the Judenrat, one of the policemen grabbed me for work. And he told me, the Germans need somebody-- because the Germans start to come in already. Because the Russian Army, the Red Army, was attacking and attacking. They came closer and closer. There were armies from all over-- Italian, Spanish, together with the Germans, fighting like brothers-- Axis. And this Jewish policeman says to me that I have to go to work to German spot over there in Zhmerinka. And I went with another couple boys. As we came to a couple of buildings there near the station, a railroad station, the German came in. They were with trucks. And they said-- they show about 10 buildings. And we have to paint this up very soon. If you're not going to finish the job, they-- he took out the gun. He's going to shoot us. And it was cold at that time. They gave us paint, and so on. And I saw that I will never do that. And German trucks already coming in. I saw a background somewhere-- running, I jumped the building. And I run out. And I run back to the ghetto. As I came-- the, the ghetto, I hide myself between the roof and ceiling there, not to get out. Because I knew that the Romanian-- the Jewish police were looking for me. They knew-- the German notify the Romanians that the boy run away. So I didn't come out. When I came out a week later, I went to the Jewish Judenrat. And they told me that I have to get 25 [NON-ENGLISH], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lashes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lashes. And that's what they did. That was by the Jewish Judenrat. And if I do that again, they'll give me to the German authorities. So that was the Judenrat. I was carrying a yellow-- all the Jews had to carry a yellow star on the back and the, the front. Like I mentioned before, the ghetto had theater, hospitals. In 1943, in the wintertime-- the end of '43, actually, yeah, the end of '43, I was loading on a train with coals on the railroad station. And I fell very, very sick. I had a temperature-- maybe 105. And I couldn't walk. And I was, like, paralyzed. My, my-- [POINTING] all my joints were, like, with knives hurting me. In the morning, they saw I, I might die. They took me to the ghetto-- they took me home. When I came home, there was a doctor they called in, actually in the ghetto, Dr. Drobner was his name. And he checked me. And he saw I have a terrible, arthritic-- they call it polyarthritis. I had polyarthritic, which means joints, infection in all my body from the cold, and so on, from the cold that I catch. And he's afraid that I might get it-- infection in the heart. And they took me to the hospital in the ghetto in Zhmerinka. And I was in that hospital three months. The only medication that I had that the doctors made was aspirin. And the aspirin was not in pills-- in liquid. It was a black-- I had to drink it. I still remember the bitterness that I drinked. And this saved my life. And so--
